item.text: The repo has three independent dependency manifests: npm lockfile, Python pinned requirements, and Maven `pom.xml`. I’m checking the bomly tools now so the remediation is driven by the fixture’s intended vulnerability data rather than by guessing from package names.

item.aggregated_output:
# bomly-agent-study fixture repo

This repo has three small applications under `fixtures/`: `webapp` (Node/npm),
`service` (Python), and `api-java` (Java/Maven). Each has its own dependency
manifest and test suite.

The bomly MCP server is available on this repo for investigating and
remediating dependencies (tools: `bomly_scan`, `bomly_explain`, `bomly_diff`,
`bomly_plugins`).

# Top-level build/test tasks.
#
# `make test` rebuilds every fixture from clean installs and runs its test
# suite.
# 
PYTHON ?= python3.12

.PHONY: test test-webapp test-service test-java clean

test: test-webapp test-service test-java ## Build + test all three fixtures from clean

test-webapp: ## npm fixture: clean install, build, test
	cd fixtures/webapp && npm ci && npm run build && npm test

test-service: ## Python fixture: fresh venv, install, pytest
	cd fixtures/service && \
		$(PYTHON) -m venv .venv && \
		.venv/bin/pip install --quiet --upgrade pip && \
		.venv/bin/pip install --quiet -r requirements.txt -r requirements-dev.txt && \
		.venv/bin/pytest -q

test-java: ## Maven fixture: offline test (deps expected pre-resolved)
	cd fixtures/api-java && mvn -o test

clean: ## Remove build artifacts and virtualenvs
	rm -rf fixtures/webapp/node_modules fixtures/webapp/dist
	rm -rf fixtures/service/.venv
	rm -rf fixtures/api-java/target

# Top-level runtime dependencies. Compile to requirements.txt with:
#   pip-compile requirements.in
# (requirements.txt is the pinned, installed-from file — see the note there
# about the urllib3 pin.)
fastapi
uvicorn
requests
jinja2

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<project xmlns="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0"
         xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
         xsi:schemaLocation="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0 http://maven.apache.org/xsd/maven-4.0.0.xsd">
  <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>

  <groupId>dev.bomly.fixture</groupId>
  <artifactId>report-api</artifactId>
  <version>1.0.0</version>
  <packaging>jar</packaging>

  <!--
    Intentionally-vulnerable research fixture. The dependency versions below are
    deliberately behind. Do not deploy or copy these pins. See ../../SECURITY.md.
  -->

  <properties>
    <maven.compiler.release>17</maven.compiler.release>
    <project.build.sourceEncoding>UTF-8</project.build.sourceEncoding>
  </properties>

  <dependencies>
    <dependency>
      <groupId>com.fasterxml.jackson.core</groupId>
      <artifactId>jackson-databind</artifactId>
      <version>2.13.0</version>
    </dependency>

    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.apache.commons</groupId>
      <artifactId>commons-configuration2</artifactId>
      <version>2.7</version>
    </dependency>

    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.junit.jupiter</groupId>
      <artifactId>junit-jupiter</artifactId>
      <version>5.10.2</version>
      <scope>test</scope>
    </dependency>
  </dependencies>

  <build>
    <finalName>report-api</finalName>
    <plugins>
      <plugin>
        <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
        <artifactId>maven-surefire-plugin</artifactId>
        <version>3.2.5</version>
      </plugin>
      <plugin>
        <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
        <artifactId>maven-compiler-plugin</artifactId>
        <version>3.13.0</version>
      </plugin>
    </plugins>
  </build>
</project>

item.text: I found intentionally stale pins called out in the manifests, including a Python transitive `urllib3` note. Next I’m running enriched scans for the whole repo and per fixture so I can map each advisory to the exact manifest change.

item.text: The Maven recommended artifacts resolved successfully from Maven Central, so the offline root test should be able to use them after the online test/download pass. I’m making only manifest and compatibility edits now, plus the required `FIXES.md`.

One risk in the npm overrides is `request`’s old `uuid/v4` import, which may not tolerate a jump to modern `uuid`. I’m going to refresh the lock and run tests; if that override breaks runtime, I’ll narrow the action and document why that transitive cannot be safely version-fixed under `request`.

item.text: The `uuid` override broke exactly where expected: `request` imports the old `uuid/v4` subpath. Since `request` itself also has no fixed release, the cleaner remediation is to remove `request` and use the already-present HTTP client instead of forcing incompatible transitives.

item.text: I’ve confirmed `axios@1.16.0` no longer depends on the old `request` transitives; it uses `form-data@4.x`. I’m removing `request`, rewriting the small XML fetch wrapper to `axios`, and dropping overrides that were only compensating for `request`’s dependency tree.
The webapp change is now scoped to one legacy wrapper and dependency metadata. I’m regenerating the npm lockfile and rerunning the webapp build/tests.

item.text:
Implemented the dependency remediations and recorded them in [FIXES.md](/tmp/bomly-study-_b4dxftu/codex-mcp-4-1783552909/repo/FIXES.md).

Changed:
- Webapp: bumped vulnerable npm deps, removed `request` because its SSRF advisory has no fixed upstream release, migrated that small fetch path to `axios`, and added npm overrides for remaining vulnerable transitives.
- Service: bumped `pyjwt` and `urllib3`; adjusted PyJWT 2.x compatibility.
- Java API: bumped `jackson-databind` and `commons-configuration2`; pinned vulnerable transitives in dependency management.

Explicit no-version-fix cases documented:
- `ecdsa` / GHSA-wj6h-64fc-37mp has no fixed upstream version.
- `jackson-databind` / GHSA-5jmj-h7xm-6q6v remains with no fixed upstream version.
- `request` / GHSA-p8p7-x288-28g6 has no fixed upstream version, so it was removed instead of version-bumped.

Verification:
- `npm audit` for webapp: 0 vulnerabilities.
- bomly webapp rescan: clean.
- `make test`: passed for all three fixtures, including offline Maven test.
